content.text: : collect a large canonical dataset, derive page records programmatically, use Claude to help build or refine the pipeline and templates, and run generation in chunked, restartable batches rather than treating the whole 9,700-page site as one monolithic AI job. For Claude specifically, the scalable strategy is to combine deterministic page generation with offline batch enrichment where needed, using Anthropic’s Message Batches API for high-volume asynchronous work because it is designed for large request sets, typically completes within about an hour, and reduces cost by 50 percent compared with standard synchronous processing.

Keith Teo’s write-up says CheckHowMuch.sg pulls more than 227,000 HDB resale transactions from Singapore’s government open data portal, generates a page for every block in Singapore, and deploys the whole thing as a static site on Cloudflare Pages using Astro and Claude Code. The key lesson is that the site’s scale comes from
, not from asking Claude to improvise 9,700 pages individually. Claude helps create and evolve the pipeline, but the final architecture behaves like a production data system.
For a site like this, the right batch strategy is

. First, assemble and clean the authoritative dataset; second, compute page-level records such as block summaries, valuations, price trends, or lease-decay metrics; third, render static pages from those records. If Claude is involved, it should enrich, normalize, summarize, classify, or repair data records in batches, not become the primary renderer of HTML.

This pattern scales because most of the 9,700 pages share the same layout and data model. Once the schema and templates exist, the marginal cost of generating one more page is mostly a data problem rather than a design problem. That is why the right strategy is batching over
, not batching over raw “make me another page” prompts.
The best batch unit is usually
one normalized page record per request
, depending on how much AI you need. Anthropic’s batch API accepts many independent Messages requests, each identified by a unique

, so each page or enrichment item can be tracked and retried independently. This is ideal for a CheckHowMuch-style site where one block page failing should not stop the other 9,699 pages from processing.
A robust CheckHowMuch-style pipeline usually splits work into four buckets:
: trends, aggregates, medians, rankings, route creation, SEO slugs.
: summaries, labels, narrative explanations, category inference, normalization.
: Astro, Next.js, Eleventy, or another SSG builds HTML from final page records.
: validation failures, missing pages, odd metrics, content anomalies.
This split keeps AI where it adds value and code where determinism matters most.
The best practical strategy is usually
for the whole corpus or major sections.
for changed records, bug fixes, or failed pages.

This is important because a real 9,700-page site is never “finished”; it is maintained, corrected, and regenerated incrementally over time. Patch mode prevents you from paying to regenerate everything for a small change.

Use full rebuild when the page schema, templates, or underlying data logic change materially. In that mode, you typically recompute all page records, batch any AI enrichment tasks, wait for results, validate outputs, and then run the static site build from the refreshed dataset. This resembles a warehouse refresh plus static publish, not a live-request AI application.

Patch generation is the real secret to maintainability. Keith Teo’s story emphasizes that many issues were edge cases discovered after launch, such as missing flat types, valuation errors, social image generation failures, and data-source changes, all of which were fixed iteratively rather than by rebuilding the product from scratch. In architecture terms, that means you want change detection, failed-item retry queues, and selective page regeneration keyed by record IDs.

Even though Anthropic’s Message Batches API supports up to 100,000 requests or 256 MB in a batch, it recommends splitting very large workloads into multiple batches for better manageability. For a 9,700-page SSG, a good chunking approach is by town, region, content type, or build shard so you can retry or inspect one slice without touching everything else. Smaller batches also make cost tracking and failure triage easier.
Anthropic’s docs are very clear that batch results may return in any order and should always be matched by
. For a CheckHowMuch-style site, use stable IDs like
so each result maps unambiguously to a page artifact or enrichment record. Good IDs are the backbone of reliable patch generation, rollback, and audit trails.

Anthropic explicitly recommends dry-running a single request shape through the regular Messages API before launching the full batch, because validation happens asynchronously for batch requests. This is especially important when you are using structured outputs or a new prompt version for thousands of pages. In the CheckHowMuch pattern, this means test one town, one block type, or one representative shard first before scaling out.
Batch generation should be versioned like code. When a prompt or schema changes, store that version in the batch request metadata or

so you know which pages were generated under which logic. This matters because a 9,700-page site may mix historical outputs, bug fixes, and evolving page models over time unless you enforce version visibility.
A batch strategy without validation is unsafe at this scale. Anthropic’s docs note that one failed request does not affect the others, and results can be

, which means your pipeline must inspect outcomes item by item rather than assuming all-or-nothing success. A strong pipeline validates every output, moves failures into a repair queue, and only promotes clean records into the final content store.

If Claude is generating any machine-consumed page data, structured outputs are a very strong fit because they constrain the model to valid schema-shaped JSON for downstream processing. This is particularly useful in batch generation because each page record can be validated automatically before it enters the static build. It reduces the chance that one malformed response breaks a whole page shard.

In the CheckHowMuch-style pattern, Claude should not be the system that decides layout per page. The page templates, visual design, navigation structure, and SEO scaffolding should be deterministic, while Claude-generated content should be slotted into known fields such as summaries, explanations, or extracted descriptions. This is how you keep 9,700 pages consistent and maintainable.

Keith Teo mentions that generating social preview images for all 9,700 blocks crashed the deploy pipeline multiple times because the image tool kept hitting memory limits at around 3,000 images. This is a strong architectural lesson: large static sites often need

for derived media like OG images, charts, and thumbnails instead of doing everything in one build step. In practice, page data generation, image generation, and final deploy should be separate stages with independent retries.

A useful production pattern is to treat the build as a set of shards rather than one indivisible deployment. For example, you can generate content shards per district or page family, validate them, then assemble the full site output once all required shards are ready. This reduces the blast radius of batch failures and helps parallelize long-running rebuilds.

Anthropic’s batch API offers 50 percent lower pricing than standard API pricing, which makes it much better suited to high-volume generation runs. For a 9,700-page site, this matters not only for the initial build but also for recurring maintenance, full refreshes, and large repair passes. If you also use shared prompt blocks, Anthropic notes that prompt caching can stack with batch discounts on a best-effort basis, further improving economics.

Most Anthropic batches complete within about one hour, but they can run up to 24 hours before expiring, and expired requests are returned individually rather than collapsing the entire job. That means a CheckHowMuch-style pipeline should be scheduled like a background data job, not like a synchronous CI step that developers wait on interactively. A practical approach is to separate “data refresh,” “AI enrichment,” and “static publish” into queued pipeline phases.
For serious scale, the cleanest orchestration pattern is usually:
Build request manifests from canonical page records.
Submit one or more Anthropic batches.
Stream results record by record from the JSONL output.
Validate and write each successful result to storage.
Trigger page rebuilds only for successful or repaired records.
The Temporal article in the results reinforces this idea by pairing Anthropic batch jobs with durable orchestration for large-scale workloads.
The most important lesson from the CheckHowMuch.sg story is not “AI can create 9,700 pages.” It is that a large site succeeds when AI is embedded in a
that can survive missing data, bad formulas, failed assets, changing upstream sources, and iterative fixes after launch. That is why the correct batch strategy is operational, not just prompt-driven.
For a CheckHowMuch-style batch generation system, the best default blueprint is:
Canonical source dataset in code-owned storage.
Deterministic page-record builder for each URL.
Claude batch enrichment for summaries, normalization, or metadata.
Validation plus repair queue for failed items.
Deterministic SSG build from only validated page records.
That is the pattern most likely to stay reliable past the first launch.

A strong concise answer is: “The CheckHowMuch.sg pattern is not one huge AI content-generation pass. It’s a batch-oriented content pipeline. You start with a canonical dataset, derive page-level records programmatically, use Claude only where interpretation or enrichment adds value, and process those enrichments in sharded asynchronous batches keyed by stable custom IDs. Then you validate results, retry failures selectively, and render the site deterministically with a static site generator. The key architectural ideas are dataset-first generation, patch-mode regeneration, batch sharding, structured outputs, and keeping AI out of the final HTML assembly step.”
